zh.lifeSummary: 當Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg於1219年出生在德國巴登貝爾可薩赤 維林根維林根時，他的父親Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg35歲，母親Adelheid von Neuffen21歲。他於1245年之前與Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen結婚。他們至少是4個兒子和3個女兒的父母。他於1250年被列為regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg。他去世於1284年1月6日在巴登阿赤 維林根維林根享年65歲，埋葬在巴登阿赤 維林根維林根。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg于1219年出生在德国巴登貝爾可薩赤 維林根維林根时，他的父亲Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg35岁，母亲Adelheid von Neuffen21岁。他于1245年之前与Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen结婚。他们至少是4个儿子和3个女儿的父母。他于1250年被列为regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg。他去世于1284年1月6日在巴登阿赤 維林根維林根享年65岁，埋葬在巴登阿赤 維林根維林根。

nameMeanings.name: Graf
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Ernst, Fritz, Gerhard, Hermann, Horst, Manfred, Udo, Alois.

German (also Gräf): status name from Middle High German grāve, grābe, which was used as a title denoting various more or less aristocratic dignitaries and officials. In later times it became established as a title of nobility equivalent to the Romance count. The vocabulary word also denoted a variety of different minor local functionaries in different parts of Germany. In the Grand Duchy of Hesse, for example, it was used for the holder of the comparatively humble office of village headman (compare Mayer , Schulz , and Vogt ). The surname could have originated from any of these senses or be a metonymic occupational or status name for a servant or retainer of a count, or a nickname for someone who gave himself airs and graces. This surname is also found in many other European countries, for example in France (Alsace and Lorraine), Hungary, Poland, Czechia, Slovakia, Croatia, and Slovenia, often as a translation into German of the corresponding Hungarian and Slavic surnames Gróf and Grof . Compare Graef .
Jewish (Ashkenazic): artificial name selected, like   Herzog   and other words denoting titles, because of their aristocratic connotations.

variants.name: Grebe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Erwin, Fritz, Hans, Willi.
German (Hesse): variant of   Graf  .

variants.name: De Graaf
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Gerrit, Adriaan, Bastiaan, Cornelis, Dirk, Geert.

Dutch: from graaf ‘count’ (see Graf ), with the addition of the definite article de ‘the’, hence a nickname for the servant of a count or for someone supposedly resembling one, and also a topographic or habitational name referring to a house or inn named with this word.

variants.name: Graefe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Armin, Arno, Goetz, Horst, Johannes.
German (North Rhine-Westphalia; also Gräfe): variant of Graf 1.

variants.name: Grefe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erhardt, Gerhard, Gerhart, Kurt.
North German: variant of   Graf  .

variants.name: Greve
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gerhard, Otto, Reinhold, Bernhardt, Claus, Erna, Erwin, Ewald, Fritz, Kurt.
North German and Scandinavian: status name from Middle Low German and Danish greve ‘count’, equivalent to German Graf .
Dutch: vowel variant of Grave ‘count’, or a topographic name from Middle Dutch greve ‘ditch, moat, channel’.

variants.name: Croff
meanings: Altered form of German   Graf   or   Graff  . Or of Dutch   Kroft  .

variants.name: Mayer
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Hans, Franz, Helmut, Wilhelm, Erwin, Gerhard, Heinz, Ilse, Lorenz, Wolfgang.

English (Staffordshire): post-medieval form of Mear, which is either a topographic name, from Middle English mere ‘pond, pool’ (Old English mere) or Middle English mere ‘boundary’ (Old English (ge)mǣre), or a habitational name from one or more of the many places with names derived from these terms, such as Mere (Cheshire, Lincolnshire, Wiltshire), Meir in Caverswall (Staffordshire), and Meare (Somerset).
English: occupational name for a physician, from Old French mire, Middle English mire, meir, meyre, originally synonymous with Myer .

variants.name: Grafe
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz, Ralf.
German (also Gräfe): variant of Graf 1.

variants.name: Graef
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Egon, Lothar, Otto, Armin, Claus, Erwin, Franz, Friedrich, Hans, Helmut, Hermann, Jochen.
German (also Gräf): see Graf .
Dutch: variant of Graaf, see De Graaf .

variants.name: Graff
meanings: German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) (also Gräff): variant of Graf . Compare Graeff .

English (London): metonymic occupational name for a clerk or scribe, from Old French grafe ‘stylus, pencil’, a derivative of Old French grafer ‘to write’ (from Late Latin grafare, from Greek graphein). It is uncertain whether the English name survived the medieval period or whether the early 17th century bearers in Britain were in fact of German origin.

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Freiburg
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Americanized form of German Freiburg or Freiberg .
Americanized form of Vreeburg, shortened variant of Vredenburg .

variants.name: Freiberg
meanings: German: habitational name from any of several places called Freiberg, from Middle High German vrī ‘free, independent’ + berg ‘mountain, hill’ (possibly an altered form of burg ‘castle, fortified town’).

nameMeanings.name: und
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Klaus, Otto, Wilhelm.

English: from the Middle English personal name Mund(e), in many cases representing Old English Munda, a pet form of names like Ēadmund (Middle English Edmund, common in East Anglia). In northern and eastern England the equivalent Old Norse Mundi (a pet form of names like Agmundr) would also have given rise to Middle English Mund(e).
German: variant of   Mundt  .

variants.name: Monday
meanings: English:

from the Middle English personal name Mundi, Mundy, usually a pet form of male personal names ending in -mund or -mond (see Mund , and compare Edmond , Hammond , and Simmonds ). Alternatively, and especially in northern and eastern England, the name might derive from Old Norse Mundi, a pet form of names like Old Norse Agmundr, Middle English Agemund.
nickname from Middle English Mone(n)day ‘Monday’. It is possibly a name given to one born on that day or to a holder of Mondayland, land held on condition of working for the lord on Mondays.

variants.name: Mundt
meanings: German:
nickname for someone with a prominent or striking mouth or whose mouth was in some other way remarkable, from Middle High German munt ‘mouth’.
nickname for a guardian or trustworthy person, from Middle High German munt ‘guardian, protector’.

variants.name: Munson
meanings: English (mainly Essex): patronymic from the personal name   Mund  , meaning ‘son of Mund’. See also   Munn  .
Americanized form of Norwegian and Danish   Monsen  . Compare   Munsen  .
Altered form of   Monson   1, a surname of Swedish origin.

nameMeanings.name: Urach,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Cheskel, Moshe, Chaim, Mendel, Pinchas, Bernat, Hershel, Meyer, Moche, Mort, Rivka, Rivky.

German: topographic name from Middle High German brache ‘fallow land, pastureland’, originally ‘newly plowed land’. Alternatively, a habitational name from any of the places called Braach (Hesse) or Brach (Baden-Württemberg, Franconia).
Jewish (Ashkenazic): variant of   Barach  .

variants.name: Breach
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Donal.
English (Sussex): variant of   Brach  .

variants.name: Brecher
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Chaim, Yosef, Baila, Chana, Ephraim, Herschel, Hershel, Meir, Meyer, Pnina, Shimon.  German Armin, Oskar, Bernd, Erwin, Gerhard.
English: variant of Brach 2, from Middle English breche, Old English brǣc, brēc ‘newly cultivated land’, the -er suffix denoting an inhabitant.
German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): from an agent derivative of German brechen ‘to break’, an occupational name for someone who crushed hemp or flax, or a nickname for a law breaker or evildoer.

variants.name: Bracher
meanings: English: from an agent derivative of Middle English brech ‘land broken up for cultivation’ (Old English brǣc, brēc), denoting a dweller by newly cultivated land. Compare Brach 2 and Breach .
Swiss German: variant of German Brachmann (see Brachman ).
German: variant of   Brach   1.

variants.name: Brachman
meanings: Americanized form of German Brachmann: variant of Brach , with the addition of Middle High German man ‘man’.

variants.name: Brech
meanings: German: variant of   Brach   1 or   Brecht  .
English: variant of   Breach   (see   Brach   2).

variants.name: Barach
meanings: Americanized form of Croatian and Serbian Barać and Barač (see Barac ).
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): acronymic surname from the first letters of the Hebrew patronymic phrase Ben Rabi Hayyim ‘son of rabbi Hayyim’.

nameMeanings.name: Heinrich
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Erwin, Helmut, Manfred, Otto, Dieter, Hans, Christl, Ewald, Gerd, Gerhard, Guenter.

German and Jewish (Ashkenazic): from the personal name Heinrich, composed of the ancient Germanic elements haim, heim ‘home’ + rīc ‘power’ (see also Henry ). In the Middle Ages this was the most popular of personal names in Germany.

variants.name: Fraise
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Henri.
American shortened and altered form of German Henrichfreise, composed of the personal name Henrich, a variant of Heinrich , and Freise .
French: probably from fraise ‘calf's caul’, used as a metonymic occupational name for a tripe butcher.

variants.name: Grossheim
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt.

German: of unclear origin. Probably an altered form of the nickname or distinguishing name grosshein ‘big Henry’ (see Heinrich ), which 17th-century records show was altered to the form of a placename.

variants.name: Heil
meanings: from a pet form of   Heinrich  .
from a short form of an ancient Germanic personal name with the element heil ‘luck’, such as Heilmuth or female Heilwig.
German:

variants.name: Heimann
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Fritz, Klaus, Otto, Alois, Ewald, Friedrich, Gerhard, Guenther, Hans, Heinz, Jurgen.
German:
from a pet form of   Heinrich  .

variants.name: Haining
meanings: Probably a variant of Heining, a Low German patronymic from Heinrich .
Scottish and English (northern; Dumfriesshire and Kirkcudbrightshire): variant of   Hanning  .

variants.name: Gean
meanings: Most probably a shortened Anglicized form of Irish Ó Géibhinn, itself a shortened form of Ó Géibheannaigh, from géibheannach ‘fettered’.
Alternatively, it could be an Americanized form of GermanGien, which is from a short form of Heinrich .

variants.name: Genrich
meanings: Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic) and German (from Russia): Russified form of Heinrich . The initial g developed since Russian has no h and alters it to g in borrowed words and names.

variants.name: Genz
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Armin, Erwin.
German: from a short form of the personal name Gen(dr)ich, Slavic form of Heinrich . Compare Gentz .

variants.name: Heindel
meanings: South German: from a pet form of the personal name   Heinrich  .

variants.name: Henry
meanings: English, French, Walloon, and West Indian (mainly Jamaica and Haiti): from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements haim, heim ‘home’ + rīc ‘power, ruler’, introduced to England by the Normans in the form Henri. During the Middle Ages this name became enormously popular in England and was borne by eight kings. Continental forms of the personal name were equally popular throughout Europe. In the period in which the majority of English surnames were formed, a common English vernacular form of the name was Harry, hence the surnames Harris (southern) and Harrison (northern). Official documents of the period normally used the Latinized form Henricus. In medieval times, English Henry absorbed an originally distinct Old English personal name that had hagan ‘hawthorn’ (compare Hain 2) as its first element, and there has also been confusion with Amery. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, e.g. German Heinrich , and also their derivatives, e.g. Swedish Henriksson (see Henrikson ). Compare Henri .
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hInnéirghe ‘descendant of Innéirghe’, a byname based on éirghe ‘arising’.
Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Éinrí or Mac Einri, patronymics from the personal names Éinrí, Einri, Irish forms of Henry. It is also found as a variant of McEnery .

meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Fürstenberg
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Arno.

German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) (Fürstenberg): habitational name from any of the many places so named, from the genitive case of Old High German furisto ‘prince’, modern German Fürst (see Furst ) + berg ‘mountain, hill’. As a Jewish surname, it is mainly artificial.

variants.name: Furst
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Eugen, Kurt, Gunter, Hans, Herta, Maximilian, Ulrich.

German and Swiss German (Fürst): nickname from Old High German furisto ‘the first, most noble one’, a cognate of Old English fyrest ‘first, foremost’. The surname Fürst is also found elsewhere in central Europe, e.g. in Czechia and Slovenia. Compare Feerst , Fierce , Fierst , First , and Fuerst .
Jewish (Ashkenazic; Fürst): artificial name from German Fürst ‘prince’. Compare Fuerst .

variants.name: Fuerstenberg
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erwin, Helmut, Otto.
German (Fürstenberg): see Furstenberg .

nameMeanings.name: Heinrich
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: from heim ‘home’ + rīc ‘power’. See Henry .

variants.name: Henry
meanings: A perennially popular given name, of Continental Germanic origin, from haim ‘home’ + rīc ‘power, ruler’. It was an Old French name, adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain. It has been borne by eight kings of England. Not until the 17th century did the form Henry (as opposed to Harry ) become the standard vernacular form, mainly under the influence of the Latin form Henricus and French Henri.

en.lifeSummary: When Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg was born in 1219, in Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Germany, his father, Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg, was 35 and his mother, Adelheid von Neuffen, was 21. He married Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen before 1245. They were the parents of at least 4 sons and 3 daughters. In 1250, his occupation is listed as regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. He died on 6 January 1284, in Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden, at the age of 65, and was buried in Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg est né en 1219, à Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Allemagne, son père, Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg, avait 35 ans et sa mère, Adelheid von Neuffen, avait 21 ans. Il a épousé Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen avant 1245. Ils ont eu au moins 4 fils et 3 filles. En 1250, sa profession de son indiquée est regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. Il est décédé le 6 janvier 1284, à Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden, à l’âge de 65 ans, et a été enterré à Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden.

de.lifeSummary: Als Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg 1219 in Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Deutschland geboren wurde, war sein Vater Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg 35 und seine Mutter Adelheid von Neuffen 21. Er hat vor 1245 Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 4 Söhne und 3 Töchter. 1250 ist sein Beruf als regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg aufgeführt. Er starb am 6. Januar 1284 im Alter von 65 Jahren in Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden und wurde in Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg è nato il nel 1219 nel Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Germania, il suo padre, Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg, aveva 35 anni e il suo madre, Adelheid von Neuffen, aveva 21 anni. Lui sposato Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen prima del 1245. Erano genitori di almeno 4 figli e 3 figlie. Nel 1250, la sua occupazione è indicata come regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. Morì il 6 gennaio 1284, nel Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden, all'età di 65 anni, e fu sepolto a Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden.

ja.lifeSummary: Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenbergが1219年にドイツバデンベズイルクサムト ヴィリンジェンヴィリンジェンで生まれた時，父Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburgは35歳，母Adelheid von Neuffenは21歳であった。1245年以前にAgnes Gräfin von Truhendingenと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも4人の息子と3人の娘の親であった。1250年，職業はregentschaft als graf von fürstenbergと記録されている。彼は1284年1月6日にバデンアムト ヴィリンジェンヴィリンジェンで65歳で死去し，バデンアムト ヴィリンジェンヴィリンジェンにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg(이)가 1219년 독일 바든 베지르크샘트 비링 비링에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg(은)는 35세였고, 그의 어머니 Adelheid von Neuffen(은)는 21세였다. 그는 1245년 이전 Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 네 명의 아들과 세 명의 딸을 두었다. 1250년, 그의 직업은 regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg이었다. 그는 1284년 1월 6일 바든 아트 비링 비링에서 65세의 나이로 사망했으며 바든 아트 비링 비링에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg nasceu em 1219, em Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Alemanha, seu pai, Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg, tinha 35 anos e sua mãe, Adelheid von Neuffen, tinha 21 anos. Ele casou-se com Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen antes 1245. Eles tiveram pelo menos 4 filhos e 3 filhas. Em 1250, sua profissão aparece como regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. Ele faleceu em 6 de janeiro de 1284, em Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden, com 65 anos, e foi sepultado em Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg родился в 1219 г. в местности виллинген, безирксэмт виллинген, баден, Германия, его отцу по имени Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg было 35 лет, а матери, которую звали Adelheid von Neuffen, 21. Он женился на женщине по имени Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen перед 1245 г. У них было по крайней мере 4 сына и 3 дочери. В 1250 году он работал по профессии regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. Он умер 6 января 1284 г. в местности виллинген, амт виллинген, баден в возрасте 65 лет и был похоронен в местности виллинген, амт виллинген, баден.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Heinrich I. Graf von Freiburg und Urach, Heinrich I. Graf von Fürstenberg nació en 1219, en Villingen, Bezirksamt Villingen, Baden, Alemania, su papá, Egino V. Graf von Urach, Egino I. Graf von Freiburg, tenía 35 años y su mamá, Adelheid von Neuffen, tenía 21 años. Se casó con Agnes Gräfin von Truhendingen antes de 1245. Fueron padres de por lo menos 4 hijos y 3 hijas. En 1250, su ocupación registrada era regentschaft als graf von fürstenberg. Murió el 6 de enero de 1284, en Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden, a la edad de 65 años, y fue sepultado en Villingen, Amt Villingen, Baden.
